questions.question: Why can't LinkedIn score all 15M job listings against a member's profile for every recommendation request?
options: Because full pairwise scoring at request time would take hundreds of seconds, so a fast candidate generation step is required first.
Because LinkedIn's database cannot store more than 1M listings at a time.
Because job listings are encrypted and cannot be read in bulk.
Because members are not allowed to see more than 100 listings per request.
questions.explanation: 15M × 950M pairwise scores is computationally infeasible at request time. Candidate generation narrows the set to ~1,000 before the heavy ranker runs.

questions.question: What problem does the LinkedIn Skills Graph solve that a simple string-match approach cannot?
options: It normalizes variant spellings and infers adjacent skills, so 'JS' and 'JavaScript' match the same listings and Python experience is credited toward ML roles.
It stores all job listings in a single sorted list by salary.
It prevents recruiters from posting duplicate listings.
It assigns a unique numeric ID to each member's skill endorsement.
questions.explanation: Skills Graph unifies synonyms (JS = JavaScript) and encodes adjacency (Python → ML), both of which are invisible to plain string matching.

questions.question: A member has skills [Python, SQL]. A job requires [Python, SQL, Spark]. What is the SkillMatchScore?
options: 0.67 — two out of three required skills matched
1.0 — all member skills are present in the job
0.5 — only half the member's skills are required
0.0 — Spark is missing so the job is filtered out
questions.explanation: SkillMatchScore = intersection / job required skills = 2/3 ≈ 0.67. The job has the final say on what is required.

questions.question: A member is at seniority level 3. A job specifies SeniorityMin=4, SeniorityMax=5. What should the seniority fit score be?
options: 0.5 — the member is one level below the minimum, which counts as adjacent
1.0 — seniority is never used as a filter
0.0 — the member is outside the range and receives no credit
0.25 — partial credit for being close to the range
questions.explanation: Level 3 is adjacent to the minimum of 4 (one step below). Adjacent seniority earns 0.5, but in this design out-of-range by one level earns 0.5, not 0.0.

questions.question: Why does LinkedIn give a score bonus to jobs with the Easy Apply flag?
options: Because Easy Apply listings historically receive 3x more applications, making them higher-quality signal for apply-rate metrics.
Because Easy Apply listings are always remote jobs.
Because Easy Apply bypasses the recruiter review step.
Because Easy Apply listings are always newer than 48 hours.
questions.explanation: Easy Apply removes friction — one-click apply increases conversion 3x. The platform incentivizes surfacing these listings higher to improve member engagement metrics.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Pinot in LinkedIn's job matching infrastructure?
options: Real-time OLAP analytics — e.g. 'top-applying jobs in your metro this week' — used for popularity signals and recruiter analytics dashboards.
Storing the primary job listing documents with full JSON payloads.
Serving precomputed member feature vectors at low latency.
Running the inverted-index candidate generation query.
questions.explanation: Pinot is LinkedIn's real-time OLAP store. It aggregates apply events to power popularity-based signals and recruiter analytics. Espresso stores the primary listing, Venice serves features, Elasticsearch does candidate generation.

questions.question: A new JavaScript framework is released. Members start self-reporting it as a skill. How should the Skills Graph handle it before it accumulates enough data?
options: Treat it as a synonym of its closest existing parent skill until it reaches a critical mass (e.g., 10K endorsements), preventing cold-start underscoring.
Block it from being added until the LinkedIn editorial team manually reviews and approves it.
Assign it a random adjacency score between 0 and 1.
Remove it from all member profiles until the official Skills Graph update cycle.
questions.explanation: Mapping a new skill to its nearest parent prevents members from being invisible to relevant job recommendations while the new skill's graph position is being established.

questions.question: Why might using hire rate as a guardrail metric be more important than apply-through rate alone when evaluating a new ranking signal?
options: A signal that increases applies but decreases hires is surfacing poor matches — it optimizes for engagement at the expense of actual member value.
Apply-through rate is not measurable in LinkedIn's A/B testing framework.
Hire rate is faster to measure than apply-through rate.
Members do not care about whether they get hired, only about discovering listings.
questions.explanation: LinkedIn's core value proposition is helping members get jobs, not just get clicks. A signal that maximizes applies but reduces hire probability is a local optimum that undermines the product.
